lessons.title: Lists Basics
lessons.concept_intro: A list holds multiple values in order. Like a shopping list — each item has a position (index), starting at 0.
lessons.analogy: A numbered list on paper. Item 0 is at the top.

lessons.title: List Methods
lessons.concept_intro: Lists have built-in superpowers: append (add), remove (delete), sort, reverse, and slicing (grabbing a piece).
lessons.analogy: Like a playlist — you can add songs, remove songs, shuffle, and play just a section.

lessons.title: Looping Through Lists
lessons.concept_intro: for item in list: lets you do something with each item. enumerate() also gives you the index.
lessons.analogy: Like a teacher calling roll — go through each name one by one.

lessons.title: Dictionaries
lessons.concept_intro: A dictionary maps keys to values. Instead of numbered positions, you use names to look things up.
lessons.analogy: Like a real dictionary: look up a word (key), get the definition (value). Or like contacts: name → phone number.

lessons.title: Nested Structures
lessons.concept_intro: Lists of dicts, dicts of lists — real data is often nested. A class roster is a list of student dicts.
lessons.analogy: Like a filing cabinet: drawers (dict) contain folders (lists) which contain papers (values).

project.title: Contact Book
project.description: Add, search, list, and delete contacts stored as dicts in a list.
